segments.s060-g81: که is the subordinating particle 'that', linking this clause to the previous line. هر روز is an adverbial phrase 'every day'. یاقوت بار آورد is a verb phrase where یاقوت (noun) modifies بار (here 'a load'); آَوَرد (past/narrative) means 'brought' or in epic style a habitual past 'would bring/was bringing'. Word order (noun + بار + verb) is typical poetic compression: 'brought a load of rubies'.
segments.s060-g82: برش here is best read as بر + ـش (a clitic 'to/for it' or 'upon it'), an archaic/poetic contracted form; its exact lemma in the provided list (cut/section) doesn't fit, so its sense is marked outside the given options. تازیان is the subject 'the Arabs'. بر کنار is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the side/edge' (بر used with the sense 'toward/to' and کنار 'edge/side'). آَوَرد is again the past/narrative 'brought' (verb agrees with the subject). The word order (object/clitic first, then subject, then prepositional phrase, then verb) is a poetic inversion for emphasis.
segments.s060-g17: This is a poetic word order meaning 'now one remedy must be made.' باید is the modal 'must/should' and is followed by the infinitive ساختن ('to make/create'), with کنون ('now') inserted before the verb for emphasis. یکی چاره literally 'one/ a certain remedy' is the logical object.
segments.s060-g18: دل و جانم (heart and my life/soul) is the thing to be relieved; م is the possessive suffix 'my' attached to جان but is not listed among the provided senses. از رنج پرداختن is an infinitival phrase 'to pay/settle/relieve from suffering' where از marks source/'from' and پرداختن is the infinitive governing the action toward رنج ('suffering').
segments.s060-g19: پرستندگان را is the indirect object marked by را; شگفت آمد is an idiom meaning 'it came as a wonder' or 'it astonished (them)'. آن here introduces the following clause (that...). The verb آمد is used impersonally/experientially ('it happened/it came').
segments.s060-g20: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. بیکاری آمد is 'idleness arose/arrived.' ز + noun (دخت ردان) gives the source/agent 'from the ...'; here ردان is adjectival/epithet modifying دخت ('the rejecting/repelling maiden'), so the clause says idleness originated from that maiden.
segments.s060-g21: همه is 'all/everyone'; پاسخش is 'its/his answer' (پاسخ + possessive suffix -ش) and را is the object marker. بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they arranged/embellished/prepared,' so the clause means 'they all set forth its answer.' The subject 'they' is expressed by the verb ending.
segments.s060-g22: چو here is temporal 'when'; اهرمن is the subject (Ahriman/the devil) and برخاستند is past plural 'they rose' or 'rose up.' از جای 'from the place' is a prepositional phrase showing source. The plural verb reflects classical/poetic agreement or a collective subject.
segments.s060-g23: که‌ای is a poetic vocative/particle used to call out 'O you...' (an archaic/exclamatory form). افسر here is used metaphorically as 'helmet/ornament/headpiece' meaning 'crown' or 'ornament of,' followed by بانوان جهان 'the ladies of the world' in genitive relation: 'the ornament of the world's women.' Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s060-g24: سرافراز is a predicate/adjectival honorific 'honored/triumphant/highly esteemed.' بر here functions in the sense 'over/among' indicating comparison or superiority, so سرافراز بر دختران مهان means 'exalted above the maidens (daughters) of the guests/visitors' — a classical poetic phrasing to praise rank among maidens.
segments.s060-g25: ستوده is a past-participle adjective meaning 'praised/celebrated' used predicatively; ز هندوستان تا به چین is a prepositional-range phrase 'from India as far as China' with ز 'from' marking source and تا ... به marking extent ('as far as/to'). Word order is poetic but straightforward: adjective + source + extent.
segments.s060-g26: میان بتان در introduces a locative comparison: 'among the betan (the beauties/idols) in(=among)'; چو is the comparative 'like/as'; روشن نگین is a noun phrase 'bright gem' where روشن qualifies نگین. The whole segment says she stands 'among the beauties like a bright gem.'
segments.s060-g27: به بالای تو بر چمن is a stacked locative phrase: به بالای تو 'on high above you' + بر چمن 'upon the meadow'; نیست is the existential negative 'there is not' applying to an implied comparator (سرو). The line means no cypress on the lawn rivals you in loftiness/beauty.
segments.s060-g28: چو here introduces a simile 'as/like'; رخسار تو is the genitival phrase 'your face/countenance'; تابش پرو is a compound 'radiance/glow of the پرو' where پرو is a poetic word for 'butterfly' (i.e., a delicate glow). The clause asserts that even the butterfly's radiance does not equal your face (تابش پرو نیست).
segments.s060-g29: نگار رخ تو is a poetic apposition meaning 'the lovely aspect of your face'; ز introduces sources 'from قنوج و رای' — two place-names (here 'قنوج' = Kannauj). و simply joins the two. Note: رای here evidently functions as a place-name, not the lexical senses given for 'rāy'.
segments.s060-g30: فرستد is a present/iterative classical form 'sends' and همی is an adverbial enclitic meaning 'ever/continually'; سوی خاور = 'toward the East'; خدای here is a vocative/exclamatory 'O God' appended for emphasis. The verb governs the whole clause: '(the beauty of your face) constantly sends (fame) eastward, O God.'
segments.s060-g31: ترا here is represented in the line by the split elements ت + را (the 2nd-person suffix + object/topic marker), functioning as an emphatic 'you'/'you yourself'; به دیده درون is a locative phrase 'in the inner eye/inward sight' (i.e., in your heart/conscience); شرم نیست is the predicate 'there is no shame.' Together: 'You yourself have no shame in your inner sight.'
segments.s060-g32: پدر را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker 'as for the father'; به نزد تو is a phrase 'in your presence/toward you'; آزرم is a noun here meaning 'reverence/respect' (the supplied sense list did not include this meaning, so it is treated as uncertain); نیست negates. The clause says there is no reverence shown to the father in the addressee's presence (i.e., you show no respect to your father).
segments.s060-g65: به آواز گفتند ما بنده‌ایم is a single clause meaning “they said aloud ‘we are servants’.” به + آواز indicates manner (by voice = aloud). گفتند is a past plural verb “they said.” ما بنده‌ایم is reported speech: ما (we) + بنده‌ایم (we are servants), where بنده‌ایم is a present-state self-identifying predicate.
segments.s060-g66: به دل مهربان و پرستنده‌ایم continues the reported speech and describes their inner stance. به دل here means ‘in (the) heart’ (به + noun = into/in), placing مهربان (=kind) as a predicative adjective; و links that adjective to پرستنده‌ایم, where پرستنده‌ایم is a present-tense plural predicate ‘we are worshippers/devotees.’
segments.s060-g67: نگه کن کنون تا چه فرمان دهی is an imperative clause. نگه کن is the familiar imperative “keep watch / look” (نگه + auxiliary کن). کنون is an adverb ‘now.’ تا introduces a purpose/indirect question here (‘so that / to see’), چه is the interrogative ‘what,’ and فرمان دهی is a subordinate clause with فرمان (command) + دهی (2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of دادن used here ‘you give’).
segments.s060-g68: نیاید ز فرمان تو جز بهی is a negation with exception. نیاید is a negative present-form meaning ‘does not/ will not come’ (here understood as ‘nothing comes’); ز فرمان تو = ‘from your command’; جز = ‘except’; and بهی is the adjective/noun ‘good/excellence.’ The whole line means nothing comes from your command except good.
segments.s060-g69: یکی گفت ز ایشان functions as ‘one (of them) said.’ یکی = ‘one/one of them,’ گفت is the past ‘said,’ and ز ایشان = ‘from them / among them.’ The rest (که‌ای سرو بن) begins the quoted address: که here introduces reported speech and ای (vocative) addresses the addressee as سرو (literally ‘cypress,’ a conventional epithet for a tall/beautiful person). بن is the first element of the imperative verb بنگر (split across the line) — i.e., the clause continues in the next segment.
segments.s060-g70: نگر تا نداند کسی این سخن completes the command and gives its purpose. نگر (imperative ‘look’ or ‘behold’) pairs with the previous بن to form بنگر; تا here is purposive (‘so that / in order that’), and نداند is the negative/subjunctive-like form ‘may not know / not come to know.’ کسی with the negative context functions as ‘anyone/no one,’ and این سخن = ‘these words / this speech.’
segments.s060-g71: اگر جادویی باید آموختن is a conditional infinitival clause. اگر = ‘if.’ جادویی here functions as the noun/adjective ‘magic / pertaining to magic’ (the thing to be learned). باید = ‘it is necessary / one must,’ and آموختن is the infinitive ‘to learn.’ Together they mean ‘if magic must be learned’ or ‘if one is to learn magic.’
segments.s060-g72: به بند و فسون چشمها دوختن states what that learning consists of. به here marks instrument/means (‘by/with’), بند = ‘fetters/bonds,’ و = ‘and,’ فسون = ‘enchantment/spell,’ and چشمها دوختن is an infinitival phrase ‘to fix/fasten the eyes’ (دوختن literally ‘to sew’ used figuratively ‘to fasten/attach one’s gaze’). The overall sense: learning consists in binding and casting spells and in fixing the gaze.
segments.s060-g9: که introduces the clause ('that'). من is the first-person pronoun. عاشقم is the adjective/noun عاشق with the enclitic ـم marking the speaker (literally 'I am a lover' / 'I am in love'); in poetry this functions as a present-state predicate. همچو is a simile particle 'like/as'. بحر دمان is a poetic phrase in which بحر 'sea' is qualified by دمان 'edge/hem' (the shore or verge of the sea). Word order places the simile phrase after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s060-g10: از او gives the source/point of origin ('from him/it'). بر شده is the compound of the prepositional/dir. element بر plus شده (past participle of شدن) to mean 'has risen/been raised' (poetic perfect). موج is the noun 'wave' (subject or focus), and تا آسمان indicates extent 'up to the sky' (تا = 'as far as/up to', آسمان = 'sky/heaven'). The verb phrase appears before/around the noun in a compact poetic order.
segments.s060-g11: پر از ... است is a poetic expression meaning 'is full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). پور سام is a name phrase 'the son/descendant of Sam' (پور = 'son/descendant', سام a proper name). روشن دلم = 'my heart is bright/clear' — دل + enclitic ـم = 'my heart' and the copula 'است' is implied/attached in the line's rhythm. The clause as a whole attributes a brightness/fullness of the speaker's heart to Pour-e-Sam.
segments.s060-g12: به خواب اندر اندیشه = 'in sleep, in thought' (به + خواب + اندر + اندیشه; poetic stacking of locatives). ز = 'from/of/about' (here marking the person of thought), و is the coordinating particle 'and/then', and نگسلم is a 1st-person verbal form built on گسل/گسستن (to part/break); here used in the sense 'I am not separated / I do not part (from him)' — poetic present emphasizing continuous attachment. The negative sense is expressed by the verbal form (poetic usage), yielding 'in dream and thought of him I am not parted.'
segments.s060-g13: همیشه is the adverb 'always'. دلم = 'my heart' (دل + ـم). در غم مهر اوست = 'is in the grief/sorrow of his love' (در غم = 'in the sorrow', مهر او = 'his love', and است/ست is the copula 'is' attached at the end). The phrase states a continuous state: the speaker's heart is always immersed in the sorrow caused by (or for) that love.
segments.s060-g14: شب و روزم = 'night and day (my ...)' — poetic fusion meaning 'night and day, in all hours' with a possessive flavour on روز/روزم. اندیشه چهر اوست = 'the thought (in me) is his face' (اندیشه = 'thought', چهر = 'face/appearance', مهر/او relations omitted here; اوست is the copula 'is'). The line means the speaker's thought is continually filled with that person's face both night and day.
segments.s060-g15: کنون = 'now'. این سخن را = direct-object/topic marking ('these words' or 'this matter' with را marking it). چه درمان کنید = 'what cure/remedy will you do/apply' — چه asks 'what kind', درمان = 'remedy', and کنید is the 2nd-person plural (or courteous) present/subjunctive of کردن used here to form a polite/future-oriented question 'will you do'. The whole asks for a proposed solution.
segments.s060-g16: چه گویید = 'what will you say' (چه = 'what', گویید is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of گفتن/گفت/گویا used as future/polite). و = 'and'. با من = 'with me' (با = 'with', من = 'me'). چه پیمان کنید = 'what pledge/covenant will you make' (پیمان = 'pledge/covenant', کنید = 2nd-person plural/polite 'will you make'). The sequence asks both for words and for a binding agreement.
segments.s060-g73: بپریم is the hortative/subjunctive form with the prefix ب- plus the verb 'پرید' (first-person-plural 'let us fly/leave'); با + مرغ is a simple prepositional phrase 'with the bird'. و links the two clauses; جادو شویم uses the subjunctive 'شویم' ('let us become') so that 'جادو' is the predicate-complement meaning 'magical/enchanted'. The two verbs are parallel hortatives: 'let us fly... and let us become...'.
segments.s060-g74: بپوییم is another hortative/subjunctive first-person-plural ('let us seek/let us go'); و again connects clauses. در چاره is a prepositional phrase where در = 'in/within' and چاره is used in the sense 'expedient/means' (a ruse or device). آهو is the noun 'gazelle' and شویم (subjunctive) makes the whole clause 'let us become (as) a gazelle' — i.e. adopt the guise of a gazelle as the expedient just mentioned.
segments.s060-g75: مگر here is used rhetorically ('why not / isn't it?') introducing a proposed action. شاه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the king' as the direct object. نزد ماه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the moon'. آوریم is first-person-plural subjunctive/hortative ('let us bring' or 'shall we bring'), so the clause reads as a rhetorical proposal to bring the king before the moon.
segments.s060-g76: به نزدیک او is a location phrase: به = 'to/toward', نزدیک = 'near', او = 'him/her' — 'to near him' (i.e. close to him). پایگاه آوریم uses پایگاه 'base/headquarters' with آوریم (hortative 'let us bring/establish') — together 'let us bring/establish a camp/base near him.' The verb آوریم repeats the hortative sense from the previous clause.
segments.s060-g77: لب سرخ رودابه places the adjective سرخ ('red') before the noun لب ('lip') and then the proper name رودابه in the genitive/attributive position: 'Rudabeh's red lip(s)'. پرخنده کرد is a compound verbal expression: پرخنده (full of laughter/smiling) + کرد (past 'made/did') — idiomatically 'broke into laughter' or 'smiled broadly.' The clause is a past, descriptive action about Rudabeh.
segments.s060-g78: رخان معصفر is a noun/adjective cluster describing the face/cheeks: رخان (soft/supple (here 'soft-faced/cheeks')) modified by معصفر ('yellowish/sallow'). سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)', بنده is 'the servant/slave' (direct object of the motion/gesture), and کرد (past 'made/did') here reports that her face turned/moved toward the servant. Grammatically, this is another past descriptive clause tied to the preceding laughter.
segments.s060-g79: که begins a subordinate clause ('for/that' or introducing the conditional). این گفته را is the object phrase 'this saying' (گفته with the object marker را). را‌گر is an archaic fused form meaning 'if' (equivalent to را اگر / if), and شوی is the present/subjunctive 'you become'. کاربند is a predicative meaning 'bound/fettered/ensnared'; together the clause reads 'if you become bound/ensnared by this saying.'
segments.s060-g80: درختی برومند کاری بلند is a nominal phrase describing the thing to be produced: درختی 'a tree', برومند 'noble/flourishing' modifies it, کاری is here the verbal-noun/infinitival sense 'to plant' (implying 'I will plant'), and بلند 'tall' further modifies the tree. The clause therefore conveys '(I will) plant a tall, flourishing/noble tree.'
segments.s060-g33: This relative clause begins with the relative/connector 'که' ('that/who') and names 'آن' ('that one') as its object (marked by 'را'). 'که اندازد از بر پدر' is literally 'who casts/throws from (از) upon/from (بر) the father' — an idiomatic poetic way to say 'who drove him out of his father's arms/side.' The verb 'اندازد' is a present/preterite-form used narratively without an overt subject here, picked up by the following main clause.
segments.s060-g34: Main clause: 'تو خواهی که گیری …' uses 'تو' + 'خواهی' ('you will/wish') with a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that/so that') plus the present-subjunctive/infinitival form 'گیری' ('you take'). The phrase 'او را' is the direct object ('him'), while 'مر' is the dative pronoun 'to/for me' (here functioning as a beneficiary/indirect object). 'به بر' = 'into/on the (your) bosom/arms.' Word order is typical Persian: subject (تو) — auxiliary/verb (خواهی) — dass- clause (که گیری …).
segments.s060-g35: 'که' again introduces a subordinate/relative idea: 'پرورده مرغ باشد به کوه' — 'پرورده مرغ' = 'a bird reared/nurtured,' with 'باشد' the 3rd-person subjunctive/present of 'to be' (here functioning as 'is/should be'). 'به کوه' indicates origin/location: 'on/at the mountain' (i.e. reared on the mountain). Adjective/noun order is poetic but follows Persian patterns (noun + modifier + copula).
segments.s060-g36: 'نشانی شده' is a passive/perfect-like compound: 'نشانی' (a mark/position/sign) + 'شده' ('has been/made'), so 'has been set/posted.' 'در میان گروه' is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the group/company/troop.' Together: 'posted/placed as a sign among the troop.'
segments.s060-g37: 'کس از مادران پیر هرگز نزاد' — 'کس' here with 'هرگز' and the negative sense yields 'no one.' 'از مادران پیر' = 'from old mothers' (maternal origin). 'نزاد' is the archaic/poetic verbal form for 'is born' (from the root زاد/زاییدن). Word order is emphatic: agent/participant phrase before the verb.
segments.s060-g38: This second clause contrasts with 'نه' ('nor') + 'ز آن کس که زاید' ('from that person who begets/gives birth') followed by 'بباشد نژاد' — 'نژاد' = 'lineage/race' and 'بباشد' is subjunctive/correlative 'would be / comes to be.' The sense: a true lineage does not arise merely from someone who produces offspring; 'ز' is poetic 'from.'
segments.s060-g39: 'چنین سرخ' = 'such red/so rosy.' The phrase 'دو بسد' is idiomatic/poetic: 'دو' = 'two' (i.e. two signs) and 'بسد' = 'suffices/is enough' (3rd-person present). The following 'شیر بوی' is inverted word order common in poetry: 'the scent (بوی) of milk (شیر).' So: 'Such redness and the smell of milk — two [signs] are enough.'
segments.s060-g40: 'شگفتی بود‌گر' is an archaic conditional turn: 'it would be wondrous/strange if…' ('بودگر' = 'if it were'/'were it so'). 'شود' is the present/subjunctive 'becomes,' and 'پیرجوی' is a compound noun 'one who seeks a pir/elder' ('pir' = old man/wise elder, '-جوی' = seeker). Together: 'It would be a marvel if he were to become a seeker of elders.' The construction uses poetic conditional phrasing rather than modern straightforward syntax.
segments.s060-g49: وز آن پس is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'from/after that'; here وز functions like the classical preposition از. به + noun repeats to mark location ('به چشم و به روی' = 'in the eyes and on the face'), with دژم (an adjective) qualifyingروی; word order places the temporal phrase first, then the locative phrases describing his expression.
segments.s060-g50: This is a compact, poetically inverted clause: ز خشم ('because of/with anger') gives the cause, اندر آورد (an older idiom: اندر + آورد = 'brought into/caused') is the compound verb, and خم is the thing brought about (a 'bend' or frown). به ابرو marks the locus ('to/into the brow'). The verb is in past/perfective form but expresses a habitual/resulting posture here.
segments.s060-g51: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said.' کاین is the contracted poetic sequence of که این ('that this' / 'that which is'), introducing the object phrase خام پیکارتان ('your crude/immature fighting'). Note پیکارتان is the noun پیکار with the 2nd-person plural possessive -تان.
segments.s060-g52: This clause has an object–verb order typical of Persian poetry: شنیدن ('hearing' / 'to be heard') is the verbal noun, گفتارتان ('your speech') is the possessor/object, and نیرزید is an archaic negative verb meaning 'is not worthy / does not merit.' Together they mean 'your speech does not deserve to be heard.'
segments.s060-g53: نه ... نه ... is a paired negative construction: نه قیصر ... نه فغفور چین = 'neither Caesar nor the Chinese faghfur.' The verb بخواهم (1st person) expresses 'I want/desire' and is placed between the two negatives (a frequent classical word order). فغفور چین means 'the faghfur (emperor) of China.'
segments.s060-g54: This continues the negative series: نه از تاجداران ایران‌زمین = 'nor of the crown‑bearers (i.e., sovereigns) of the land of Iran.' از introduces the partitive/relational phrase, and تاجداران is the plural of تاجدار 'crown‑bearer' (i.e., crowned ruler).
segments.s060-g55: به بالای من = 'on/above me' (literally 'to the top/side of me'); پور سامست is a compact poetic phrase where سامست represents سام + است ('is [of] Sam'), so پور سامست زال reads 'Zal is the son of Sam' (word order inverted for meter: 'the son of Sam is Zal').
segments.s060-g56: This string lists Zal's attributes introduced by what is effectively 'he with…' (in the manuscript the pronoun + با fuse as ابا); بازوی شیر = 'the arm/strength of a lion' (a heroic metaphor), and با برز و یال = 'with great/exalted might and a mane' (برز = high/exalted; یال = mane/crest), all coordinated to praise his physical power and heroic aspect.
segments.s060-g41: Syntactically this is a nominal predicate followed by a copula: 'جهانی' functions as a predicate noun 'a world', modified by 'سراسر پر از مهر' 'entirely full of affection'. The final 'تست' is an archaic 2nd-person copula ('thou art' / 'you are') addressing the beloved; word order places the predicate before the copula, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s060-g42: 'به ایوانها' is a locative/benefactive phrase ('to/for the iwans/halls') and introduces the place or the things being adorned. 'صورت چهر' is a stacked nominal phrase ('the form/appearance — the face'), two near-synonyms in apposition emphasizing visage. The trailing 'تست' again is the 2nd-person copula 'you are' (poetic), so the clause says that you are the face/ornament for the halls.
segments.s060-g43: The phrase 'ترا' (here split into enclitic 'ت' + 'را') marks the addressee ('to/for you'). 'با چنین روی و بالای و موی' is an instrumental/causal phrase introduced by 'با' ('with, by means of'), listing attributes: 'روی' (face), 'بالای' (bearing/stature; 'highness' used as noble bearing), and 'موی' (hair). The coordination with و links parallel attributes describing the person.
segments.s060-g44: 'ز چرخ چهارم' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from the fourth sphere/heaven'. 'خور' is 'the sun' and is the subject that (poetically) 'comes' upon the addressee. 'آیدت' is a compound verb form 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت). The final 'شوی' is an archaic optative/auxiliary sense (‘may it be’ / 'may you be'); together the clause expresses a wish: 'may the sun of the fourth heaven come upon you.'
segments.s060-g45: 'چو' here = 'when', introducing a temporal clause. Word order places the subject 'رودابه' before the object phrase 'گفتار ایشان' ('their speech/words') and ends with the past tense verb 'شنید' ('she/he heard'). The object phrase uses 'ایشان' as 'they/their' referring to the speakers.
segments.s060-g46: Again 'چو' = 'when'. 'از' here marks source or cause ('from' / 'by'). 'باد آتش' is a poetic compound 'wind of fire' (the burning wind), where 'آتش' is used as 'passion/flame'. 'دلش' is the possessed noun 'her heart' (دل + enclitic ش = her). 'بردمید' is a past transitive verb meaning 'seized/took away' — so the clause says the fiery wind seized her heart (i.e., passion overcame her).
segments.s060-g47: 'بر ایشان' ('upon them') gives the direction/target of the action. 'یکی بانگ برزد' literally 'one cry struck/rose' — 'یکی' ('a certain/one') + 'بانگ' (a cry) + 'برزد' (an archaic past verb meaning 'came/fell/was uttered'), so the clause means 'a cry came upon them.' 'به خشم' is an adverbial phrase 'with anger' specifying manner.
segments.s060-g48: 'بتابید' here is a past tense verb from تابیدن; in context it means 'she turned/bent' (her face) rather than 'shone'. The object 'روی' is 'face'. 'و' links the next action. 'بخوابید چشم' uses 'بخوابید' (literally 'caused to sleep' / 'rested') with 'چشم' (eye) — poetically 'she closed her eyes' or 'lowered her gaze.' Both verbs are past tense, describing sudden physical motions in anger.
segments.s060-g1: ورا is an archaic/dialectal sequence meaning ‘to him/with him’ (equivalent to به او / او را) marking possession or accompaniment. پنج ترک پرستنده بود is a nominal clause with عدد + قوم + وصف + بود: “there were five Turk worshippers” — بود is the past copula anchoring the description in past time.
segments.s060-g2: This is a second nominal predicate describing the same person. پرستنده و مهربان بنده is a coordinated noun+adjective phrase (“a worshipper and a kind servant”); بود is again the past copula (‘was’). The subject is omitted because it is carried over from the prior clause.
segments.s060-g3: بدان is an older dative phrase (به + آن) meaning “to those” or “to that/those”; here it marks the indirect object. بندگان خردمند is the indirect object phrase ‘wise servants’. گفت is the past-tense verb ‘(he) said’ introducing reported speech; the actual speaker is omitted and understood from the context.
segments.s060-g4: که is the complementizer ‘that’ introducing the reported clause. بگشاد is an older verbal form meaning ‘to open/reveal’ (here a perfective/stative root form); in classical Persian the finite auxiliary often follows the main verb. خواهم is the first‑person present/future auxiliary ‘I will / I intend’, so بگشاد خواهم = ‘I will reveal’. نهان از نهفت is a tautological phrasing: نهان ‘hidden/secret’ + از نهفت ‘from what is concealed’ — both words reinforce the idea of something concealed.
segments.s060-g5: شما is the 2nd‑person plural/formal subject ‘you’. یک به یک is an adverbial phrase ‘one by one’ (به here gives the ‘by/‑ly’ sense). رازدار is the predicative noun ‘confidant’. منید is a classical combination of the possessive/agentive element من (‘of me’/‘my’) plus the 2pl ending ید, functioning here as ‘you are mine’ (i.e. ‘you are my …’); the copula ‘to be’ is built into this archaic form.
segments.s060-g6: This repeats the predicative pattern with coordination: پرستنده و غمگسار are two coordinated nouns/adjectives ‘worshipper and consoling‑companion’. The trailing منید again marks possession + 2nd‑person plural copular sense (‘you are my …’). The coordinate noun phrase is the complement of that copula.
segments.s060-g7: بدانید is the imperative/plural present ‘know’ (addressing listeners): ‘Know this’. هر پنج means ‘each of the five’. و آگه بوید links an additional condition/remark ‘and if (he) is aware’ — آگه ‘aware’, and بوید is an archaic present/optative form of بودن here meaning ‘is’ or ‘would be’. The verb form is classical rather than modern.
segments.s060-g8: همه‌ساله is an adverb ‘every year’. با بخت همره is a prepositional phrase ‘together with / accompanied by Fortune’ (با + بخت, with همره reinforcing ‘together/along with’). بوید again is the archaic copular/predicative form ‘is/will be’, so the whole clause says they will be with Fortune yearly.
segments.s060-g57: This clause is a poetic conditional: گرش is an archaic/contracted conditional particle + clitic (roughly = 'if he/...'), پیر خوانی is a present-second‑person verbal phrase 'you call (him) old' (خوانی = you call/say), and همی‌گر + جوان gives the result 'he would be young' (همی‌گر here marks the conditional/result). Word order shows the condition first and the concluding predicate (جوان) last, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s060-g58: مرا here marks the experiencer/beneficiary ('to me' / 'for me'); او is the (grammatical) subject 'he'; به جای + جای = 'in place of / instead of'; تنست is a fused copular phrase (تن است) 'is (a) body' with the copula attached to تن, and و روان links the companion noun 'soul'. Together it says 'to me he is in the place of body and soul' (i.e. he is my whole self).
segments.s060-g59: مرا + مهر او functions as 'his love for me' (مرا marking the target/beneficiary of مهر او). دل ندیده is the subject phrase 'the unseeing / inexperienced heart' (ندیده = 'unseen, inexperienced'), and گزید is the simple past 'chose'. The verb therefore says 'the inexperienced heart chose his love for me (without seeing)'.
segments.s060-g60: همان دوستی = 'that very friendship' (همان emphasizing identity). از شنیده is a prepositional phrase 'from/heard (things)' (i.e. on the basis of hearsay), and گزید repeats the past verb 'chose'. The line contrasts 'choosing without seeing' with having chosen 'from what was heard'.
segments.s060-g61: بر او مهربانم = 'I am kind/affectionate toward him' (مهربانم = adjective + 1st‑person suffix 'I am kind'). به بر روی و موی is a prepositional phrase 'for/on account of (his) face and hair' (به ... بر on the basis of his visage and locks), giving the reason or motive for the speaker's affection.
segments.s060-g62: به سوی هنر is a directional phrase 'toward excellence/virtue/skill'; گشتمش is the past form with enclitic -ش ('I sought/turned to him' or 'I turned (to) — him' depending on context; here 'I turned/searched for him'), and مهرجوی is a noun/adjective 'seeking affection' or 'lover' describing the speaker's stance. The clause says the speaker moved toward virtue and sought him as one seeking love.
segments.s060-g63: پرستنده is subject 'the devotee/adorer'; آگه شد is a two‑word predicate 'became aware' (آگه = aware, شد = became); از راز او = 'of his secret' uses از with the genitive. The clause means 'the worshipper/devotee became aware of his secret.'
segments.s060-g64: چو + بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = when; بشنید = he heard). دل‌خسته is an adjectival noun 'the weary / heart‑sore (heart)', and آواز او = 'his voice/utterance'. The whole temporal clause modifies the previous verb: 'when the weary heart heard his voice.'

sentences.s060-s41: that every day it brought a load of rubies, and it brought the Arabs to its edge.
sentences.s060-s9: Now one remedy must be made: to rid my heart and life of suffering.
sentences.s060-s10: It was a wonder to the worshippers that idleness came from the rejecting maiden.
sentences.s060-s11: They all prepared its reply, when Ahriman rose from his place.
sentences.s060-s12: O you helmet/ornament of the world's ladies, exalted above the maidens of the guests!
sentences.s060-s13: Praised from India to China, she is a bright gem among the beauties.
sentences.s060-s14: On the lawn above you there is no cypress; no butterfly's radiance is like your countenance.
sentences.s060-s15: The beauty of your face sends fame from Kannauj and Ray ever toward the East, O God.
sentences.s060-s16: You yourself have no shame in your inward sight; you show no reverence for your father before you.
sentences.s060-s33: They said aloud, "We are servants; in our hearts we are kind and devoted worshippers."
sentences.s060-s34: Watch now to see what command you will give; nothing will come from your command except good.
sentences.s060-s35: One of them said, "O cypress, look—so that no one may learn these words."
sentences.s060-s36: If magic must be learned, it is to bind and enchant, and to fix the eyes.
sentences.s060-s5: That I am in love — like the sea's edge; from him a wave has risen up to the sky.
sentences.s060-s6: My heart, bright, is full of the son of Sam; in sleep and in thought of him I am not parted.
sentences.s060-s7: My heart is always in the sorrow of his love; night and day my thought is his face.
sentences.s060-s8: Now what remedy will you apply to these words? What will you say, and what covenant will you make with me?
sentences.s060-s37: Let us fly with the bird and become magical; let us go and, as a ruse, become a gazelle.
sentences.s060-s38: Why not bring the king before the moon, and set up a camp near him?
sentences.s060-s39: Rudabeh's red lips broke into laughter; her soft, sallow face turned toward the servant.
sentences.s060-s40: For if you become ensnared by this saying, (I will) plant a tall, noble tree.
sentences.s060-s17: You — the one who cast him from his father's arms — will you now wish to take him into your own?
sentences.s060-s18: That he be a bird reared on a mountain, set as a sign amid the troop.
sentences.s060-s19: No one is ever born of aged mothers; nor does lineage come from that one who merely brings forth.
sentences.s060-s20: Such a red flush and the smell of milk are two signs enough; it would be astonishing if he became a seeker of elders.
sentences.s060-s25: From that time on, with sullen eyes and face, he knit his brow in anger.
sentences.s060-s26: He said: 'This crude fighting of yours — your speech does not deserve to be heard.'
sentences.s060-s27: I desire neither Caesar nor the Chinese faghfur, nor the crown‑bearers of the land of Iran.
sentences.s060-s28: Above me is Zal, son of Sam — a man with the arm/strength of a lion and with great might and a mane.
sentences.s060-s21: The whole world is filled with affection; you are the face that adorns the halls.
sentences.s060-s22: Because of such a face, bearing, and hair of yours, may the sun of the fourth heaven come upon you.
sentences.s060-s23: When Rudabeh heard their words, the wind-born fire seized her heart.
sentences.s060-s24: A cry rose upon them in anger; she turned her face and closed her eyes.
sentences.s060-s1: There were five Turk worshippers with him; he himself was a worshipper and a kind servant.
sentences.s060-s2: He said to those wise servants that I will reveal what is hidden.
sentences.s060-s3: You, one by one, are my confidants; you are my worshippers and my consolers.
sentences.s060-s4: Know that each of the five — and if he is aware — will be accompanied by Fortune every year.
sentences.s060-s29: Even if you call him old, he is (still) young; to me he stands in place of body and soul.
sentences.s060-s30: My inexperienced heart chose his love for me unseen; it chose that same friendship from what it had heard.
sentences.s060-s31: I am affectionate toward him for his face and hair; I turned toward excellence and sought him, a seeker of love.
sentences.s060-s32: The worshipper became aware of his secret when the weary heart heard his voice.
